text: The surgeon visually inspects the pelvic organs, identifying two intramural fibroids, one pedunculated subserous fibroid, and an additional small anterior fibroid.

The surgeon injects diluted vasopressin at a 1:200 concentration into the uterine tissue using a pisat needle. After inserting the needle, the surgeon aspirates to check for blood, indicating the need to change the injection site if blood is present.

it is very vital to constantly see on the pulse oximeter and multipara while injecting and you should in prior inform your anesthesiologist for any changes and error put a word of caution with them in most of the cases when fibroid size is less than ten centimeters eighty to hundred cc volume is sufficient effect of any vasoconstrictor last for three to uh comes within three to four optimal effect comes within three to four minutes itself and lasts for around forty minutes to an hour so when you want to take out multiple fibroids it is always better to go myoma by myoma
The surgeon prepares to inject vasopressin into nearby myomas that are anticipated to be removable within twenty minutes, ensuring effective vasoconstriction for fibroid removal.

The surgeon utilizes an umbilical optical port and a midline port, both measuring ten to eleven millimeters, for fibroids less than ten centimeters. An eleven-millimeter myomascrew is employed to manipulate the fibroid, demonstrating its sturdiness for subsequent morcellation through the same port.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw inserted through the midline port to apply traction on the fibroid towards the umbilical valve, ensuring that any slippage of the screw will exit through the abdominal valve without damaging vital structures.

The surgeon incises the uterine wall using a monopolar device, carefully addressing small feeding vessels before detaching the fibroid. In cases of subserous fibroids, the surgeon shells out the thin myometrial layer covering the fibroid, while preserving the myometrium in deep intramural fibroids for better future outcomes. The fibroid is mostly external, allowing for some sacrifice of the thin myometrial layer.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully separate the fibroid fibers, avoiding unnecessary tearing. A myoma screw is employed to apply constant traction, but due to the difficulty of inserting a large myoma screw, a sturdy tenaculum is used as an alternative instrument.

The surgeon detaches the fibroid from the myometrium, carefully managing the feeding vessels and small fibers at the base to ensure hemostasis. Once the lesion is completely detached, the surgeon secures hemostasis without the need for sutures.

The surgeon uses a monopolar electrosurgical instrument set between 45 to 50 for pure cutting current to make an incision in the myometrium, ensuring adequate depth for proper enucleation of the fibroid.

The surgeon grasps the fibroid using either a sharp tenaculum or a myoma screw, applying traction to detach it from the base. Hemostasis is secured using small bursts of energy at low settings once the fibroid is nearly detached.
The surgeon easily removes the fibroid using the same procedure, repeating the incision on another myoma.

On myometrium, for myomectomy, there are a lot of controversies associated. Some school of thought says that you should avoid using energy. You should use mechanical energy only. Yes, you always have an option of using mechanical energy. You can always avoid energy.

The surgeon discusses the use of monopolar cautery on pure cutting current for mechanical cutting, suggesting cold scissors as an alternative for incisions on the myoma. The incision should be adequate to at least sixty percent of the myoma's widest diameter to facilitate removal through the myometrial incision without damaging surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the third sub-serous anterior fibroid, paying close attention to the bladder margins. Traction is applied using a sharp instrument or myoma screw to create space between the uterus and the fibroid, ensuring the dissection remains flush to the fibroid to prevent injury to the myometrium and bladder.

The surgeon uses a pure cutting current to detach the fibroid from the uterus, carefully managing small oozes to prevent capillary retraction and bleeding. Traction is applied to keep the fibroid away from the bladder and uterus, ensuring minimal damage to surrounding tissues.

The surgeon addresses a bleeding defect in the myometrium by suturing it using an extracorporeal knot tying technique with a PDS monofilament suture. A 30 mm curved needle is employed, inserted through an 11 mm valveless trocar. Two needle holders are used, one from the central port and another from the lateral port, to facilitate precise suturing.

The surgeon uses a toothed needle holder and grasper to secure tissue on the right side. After passing the needle through the tissue, a knot pusher is used to tighten the suture. A 90 cm thread is utilized to take two box stitches, leaving 40 to 45 cm of thread for intracorporeal suturing. Various methods, including simple braided dissolvable sutures, are available for closing the defect.
